headlines.title: Microsoft breaks ground on new data center in San Jose
headlines.description: Microsoft broke ground Wednesday on a 48-megawatt data center campus in San Jose’s Alviso neighborhood that city leaders say will support growing demand for cloud computing and artificial intelligence. The project will be Microsoft’s first purpose-built, company-owned and operated data center in San Jose, according to Jonathan Noble, Microsoft’s senior director of government affairs. John Kane, senior vice president at the HITT Contracting firm working on the project, said the facility will employ more than 600 workers at peak construction. San Jose Mayor Matt Mahan said the data center will support more than 100 ongoing jobs once operational. Mahan described data centers as “critical infrastructure” needed to support modern services, including healthcare, transportation and artificial intelligence. “You need this backbone of infrastructure to power the modern world,” Mahan said. Noble said the facility will support digital systems people rely on every day, including online transactions, hospitals, medical records, and 911 systems. City officials said the project will use 100% recycled water for cooling and renewable energy, and will be able to supply power back to the electrical grid during periods of high demand. Kane said construction is expected to be completed in about 18 months. Gilroy May 30 Artificial intelligence Apr 28 Artificial intelligence May 13 State pondering regulations amid AI boom

headlines.title: Oakland man charged after allegedly shooting into SUV with kids inside
headlines.description: A 26-year-old Oakland man is facing several felony charges after allegedly shooting into a car full of people, including children, on Saturday. Kamani Jermaine Taylor has been charged with six counts of assault with a semiautomatic firearm, child abuse, shooting at an occupied vehicle, shooting at an unoccupied vehicle and two misdemeanor firearm brandishing charges, according to the Alameda County District Attorney’s Office. Taylor allegedly opened fire at an SUV in the 1200 block of 78th Avenue in East Oakland Saturday. Six people, including children, were in the SUV at the time. He was arrested in Oakland at about 11 p.m. that same day. Prosecutors didn’t disclose a possible motive for the shooting. Taylor is scheduled for an arraignment at 9 a.m. Thursday in Alameda County Superior Court.

headlines.title: Hall of Famer Chris Wondolowski on what makes the World Cup special
headlines.description: Chris “Wondo” Wondolowski is a soccer legend, playing most of his career with the San Jose Earthquakes. On May 1, he was inducted into the National Soccer Hall of Fame. During his MLS career, Wondolowski played five years with Team USA, including the 2014 World Cup in Brazil. He called that experience one of the highest and lowest of his career. Wondolowski sat down with NBC Bay Area’s Raj Mathai to talk about his career, the beautiful game and how the World Cup can unite everyone, soccer fan or not. Watch their conversation in the video player above. soccer Jun 9 soccer Jun 8 East Bay family's deep ties with the Oakland Roots soccer team

headlines.title: San Jose prepares for tens of thousands of fans ahead of World Cup watch parties
headlines.description: With the countdown to the World Cup matches down to its final hours, host areas like the Bay Area are buzzing with frantic, last-minute preparations for massive watch parties. Organizers are putting the finishing touches on the official downtown San Jose watch party at San Pedro Square, a joint effort between the city and the San Jose Earthquakes. The event features artificial turf and two massive television screens, including one measuring 27 feet. Tens of thousands of fans are expected to flood the downtown area. “I think the spirit and the mood are going to be incredibly exciting,” said Jared Shawlee, president of the San Jose Earthquakes. “We’ve already had over 20,000 RSVPs for the game tomorrow, Mexico vs. South Africa at noon. We’re at 15,000 RSVPs for the USA game on Friday night, so the mood will be incredible.” The San Pedro Square event is designed to create a central gathering spot for soccer fans while giving a major economic boost to downtown businesses. Local business owners say the tournament’s scale sets it apart from other major sporting events. “I think, you know, for the U.S. games… the big teams like Mexico, Colombia, England, Germany, Italy… I think it’s gonna be, like, big,” said David Mulvehill, a partner at O’Flaherty’s Irish Pub. “I think those games gonna draw a huge crowd… and obviously the bars are gonna be full on San Pedro.” For local families, the community atmosphere is a draw, but the true excitement lies in the tournament’s high stakes. “There’s all these countries… and it’s really fun to watch because it’s so competitive,” said Joseph Quiroz of San Jose, adding that he is rooting for Spain. While San Pedro Square is expected to be a primary destination, it will not be the only place for fans to gather. Neighborhood watch parties are being organized throughout the South Bay. The Directors’ Alliance San Jose is coordinating events across 11 business districts, including Willow Glen, Alum Rock, Japantown, and the Monterey corridor. “You can enjoy cultural events,” said Helen Masamori, co-director of the Directors’ Alliance San Jose and representative of the Alum Rock Business Association. “We’re gonna have dance, music, food, and each district… 11 of us… are gonna have their own watch parties.” Because the tournament spans more than a month, organizers are encouraging fans to explore different venues across the city. “There’s so many games… over a period of over a month,” said Shyam Panchal of the Monterey Corridor Improvement District. “Absolutely go to San Pedro Square… come down to the Plant… go down to the Mexican Heritage Plaza… go where you wanna see and have a new adventure, a new journey.” Officials urge attendees to check the websites of the San Jose Earthquakes, the city of San Jose or local business districts to RSVP and find information on what items are permitted at the venues.

headlines.title: Body found at waste facility in San Jose
headlines.description: San Jose police are investigating after a deceased person was found Wednesday afternoon at a waste facility in North San Jose. At approximately 12:50 p.m., officers responded to a report of a deceased person at a waste facility located in the 700 block of Los Esteros Road, according to the San Jose Police Department. Police said they are working with the Santa Clara County Medical Examiner’s Office to determine the circumstances surrounding the death. No additional information was immediately available, and the investigation remains active and ongoing. This is a developing story. Please check back for updates.

headlines.title: BART Transbay Tunnel mishaps tied to disabling of surge protection system
headlines.description: A root cause analysis of a string of smoky incidents and fires inside the Transbay Tube last year concluded that one likely contributing factor was BART’s failure to reactivate an electrical surge projection system that had been deactivated during seismic retrofit work in the underwater tunnel. The root cause analysis covered eight mishaps that began in August of 2025 and continued until the end of the year. One likely contributing factor, the analysis said, was that the tunnel’s power surge protection system had been deactivated during many of the incidents. During a six year seismic project, BART removed so-called cross bonds – wires that function to share power by linking the two sets of tracks running through the Transbay Tube. The bonds were removed to allow workers to move safely around rails that would otherwise be energized. But after that work was done in 2024, BART left the system disconnected, the consultants found. Without the power being shared with the cross bonds, the system could be more vulnerable to the string of still-unexplained power surges that began in the summer of 2025. Last November, following the first of what became a total of eight subway incidents, BART restored the disabled power balancing system. And at a BART board briefing in January, BART General Manager Bob Powers took responsibility for not reviving the disabled system. “They should have been put back with that project, and there’s no two ways about that. And we’re owning that,” Powers said. “Those bonds should have been put back in.” Beyond the deactivated power balancing system, experts had highlighted another possible factor in all the mishaps — BART’s failure to clean its insulators – ceramic devices designed to block power surges from flowing from the high voltage Third Rail to the ground. But the independent analysis did not point to insulator failure as a root cause of the tunnel mishaps. BART initially identified a blown insulator in the first of the incidents in the Transbay Tube, when smoke filling the tunnel forced a train to halt on Aug. 29. Blown insulators were identified in several of the subsequent incidents. Back in 2015, BART blamed the build-up of metallic dust on subway insulators as causing flashover incidents. The agency ordered the ceramic devices cleaned of the dust known to cause short-circuiting and fires on insulators. But in 2020, BART stopped cleaning its insulators, citing the risk that they could become damaged from the dry ice crystals used to clean them. Although the consultants in the report on the more recent events discounted blown insulators as a root-cause, their findings indicated environmental conditions – potentially including caked on dust — may make insulators more susceptible to failing. The team’s inspection of the blown insulators found “normal aging and environmental exposure expected within tunnel installations,” according to the summary of the findings. By this year, BART had resumed cleaning dust from the insulators using soap and water. Separately, the analysis identified another act by BART that may have played a role in the incidents. It noted that BART “disabled” aging sensors designed to protect the system by shutting down circuits during power surges. BART turned off the advanced protection measures, however, hoping to avoid nuisance trips of the breaker system, as a result, “relying only on basic breaker protection,” according to the study. The outside engineers noted that BART has since upgraded that circuit protection system by installing new equipment to better protect the system against power surges. Still, the final report summary to the BART board says the agency continues to face a challenge in setting protective systems to both protect against fires and prevent “nuisance” trips.

headlines.title: Police offers $250K reward for information in string of ‘Doodler' murders
headlines.description: Investigators are trying to get people to come forward with information about a string of killings in the 1970s in San Francisco. Authorities said the six victims were murdered on and around Ocean Beach and Lands End between January 1974 and June 1975. It is believed they were targeted because they were gay. Cold case detectives say the murders were by a single killer dubbed “The Doodler”, because he reportedly sketched his victims prior to the killings. SFPD is offering a $250,000 – the maximum offered in cold cases. Those with information are encouraged to call SFPD.

headlines.title: Police investigate Berkeley Animal Shelter fire as arson
headlines.description: A fire that forced the Berkeley Animal Shelter to close over the weekend is now being investigated as arson. The Berkely Animal Shelter is still closed days after a suspicious fire forced all of its animals to be relocated. Now, police say they’ve determined the fire was intentionally set. “They have no way of escaping, no way of getting out. It seems like the worst kind of violent crime, animals are unconditional love,” said Veronica Rusell, who works nearby. The fire happened around 10 p.m. Saturday, on the staircase inside the front of the building. Berkeley police say a window of the shelter was broken to gain access inside. Wes Hoye is a former volunteer at the shelter. He came to the shelter Wednesday, hoping they would help him foster Puff the pitbull and help her find a permanent home. Now, he’s concerned about the impact the closure could have on animals throughout the region. “I think they should get every bit of time served as possible,” he said. “Now, all shelters that are probably like, underfinanced, overpopulated with animals are getting more animals, and it is not fair to the animals crammed in this situation and it’s not fair for the people working there.” No people or animals were injured. Officials say the building’s sprinkler system helped contain the fire before it could spread. Investigators are looking into how many people may be involved. “From the video we can see there were people in and around the building and threw some item or object whatever it was at the window causing it to break and then the fire started,” said Berkeley police Officer Byron White. City officials say there is no structural damage to the building, but the impact is still being felt. The shelter will remain closed while the city works on a restoration plan, possibly for weeks or even months. All of the animals have been moved to partner organizations. “I don’t know what their intentions were. I know our animal care services in our city are beloved and it is hard to understand why somebody would want to do this,” White said. Berkeley police have not released any information on a suspect or suspects involved. They are asking anyone with information or video to contact them to help lead to an arrest.

headlines.title: Worker rescued after falling in building under construction in San Francisco
headlines.description: San Francisco firefighters rescued a worker who fell inside a building under construction Wednesday afternoon, authorities said. The fire department said they responded to the 2300 block of Washington Street at around 12:55 p.m. after receiving a call reporting that a construction worker fell inside a building under construction. According to fire officials, the worker was rescued and extracted from the second floor of the building, down to an aerial ladder and then to the ground. The worker was taken to a local trauma center, authorities said.

headlines.title: South County youth farmers protest Santa Clara County Fair date change
headlines.description: Santa Clara County fairgrounds leaders caused an uproar this year for pushing the annual fair date to keep vendors happy and — as a consequence — some youth farmers out of contention for junior livestock shows. The parents’ response: Fine, we’ll organize our own. Preparations for an alternative fair are now underway among farmers in South County, one of the South Bay’s last remaining agricultural vestiges, as a form of protest against the county fair’s new Aug. 19-23 dates this year. All elementary through high school age students participating in two agricultural student programs — 4-H and Future Farmers of America — can show their cattle, sheep, goats, pigs, horses and chickens at the new “South Valley Ag Fair.” The alternative event will run July 27 and July 28 at Thorson’s Arena in San Martin with an auction on Aug. 1 at Rancher’s Choice Stockyard in Aromas, off Highway 101. “I grew up in San Jose and have attended the Santa Clara County Fair since the late 1970s,” Megan Davies, a parent of three Gilroy High School farming students who’s supporting the new fair, told San Jose Spotlight. “(The county fair) is not an event my family will be supporting moving forward.” Organizers didn’t have the details yet on operating costs, funding or expected attendance for the alternative event. But they said sponsorships are steadily flowing in with official backing from Future Farmers of America and support from Cal Poly San Luis Obispo students and faculty who run the Western Bonanza Junior Livestock Show — considered the “Best in the West” for such events — in Paso Robles every year. They added the Cal Poly Livestock judging team will be involved. “Many of the livestock families and FFA chapters as well as some local 4-H clubs have come together and we have created our own ‘Fair’ as a solution to our current situation,” Monica Simon, a former FFA member whose child is in the program in Morgan Hill, told San Jose Spotlight. “I think the Santa Clara County Fair will look much different this year as well as in the future as a result of our new fair and community support we are receiving.” Santa Clara County Fairgrounds Executive Director Salene Duarte is taking the alternative fair in stride. “We really don’t see the South Valley Ag Fair as competition,” Duarte told San Jose Spotlight. “Instead, we think it’s another great place for young people to show their animals and keep building their skills–whether that’s caring for animals, being a leader, keeping records or speaking in public.” The county fair has traditionally happened in the month of July. Duarte has said the new August dates are meant to accommodate post-pandemic shifts to fair vendors’ schedules, and will likely be the date for future years. The change was announced last December, months after youth farmers — some of whom will start college out of town by the new date — had already purchased cattle and bred sows. Parents said that would have forced youth farmers to balance their participation in the junior livestock shows with their first week of school. Parents said the date change would also affect some youth finalizing their farmer degrees. It also possibly meant three extra weeks of financial burden to feed and care for livestock. “These kids and teens are our future farmers, ag teachers and veterinarians,” Davies said. “It is with pride that I fully support this amazing coming together of the community for our agriculture youth.” This story was originally published by San Jose Spotlight. sanjosespotlight.com/south-county-families-create-their-own-livestock-fair

headlines.title: PG&E implements North Bay power shutoffs amid heat, high winds
headlines.description: PG&E is implementing Public Safety Power Shutoffs (PSPS) as high winds continue across parts of the North Bay. According to PG&E’s outage map, nine counties are affected by the proactive power shutoffs. Three of those counties are in the Bay Area: Napa, Sonoma and Solano. Napa County has the highest number of affected customers, with more than 900 impacted by the shutoffs. Sonoma County has nearly 630 affected customers, while Solano County has just over 100. While the number of affected customers is lower in Solano County, residents there say the outages still have an impact. “All our water is on well so when the electricity goes out, guess what?…we can’t take a shower as long as it’s off,” said Vacaville rancher John Pierson. PG&E spokesperson Tamar Sarkissian said the utility only uses Public Safety Power Shutoffs when necessary. “Public safety shutoffs are the last resort…if it’s not necessary we are not going to call a PSPS,” Sarkissian said. PG&E said the shutoffs began around 9 p.m. Wednesday and are expected to continue in Napa, Sonoma and Solano counties until 4 p.m. Thursday. The National Weather Service has also issued a Red Flag Warning for the East Bay Hills and North Bay Interior Mountains through 9 a.m. Thursday. PG&E Jun 10 wildfires 10 hours ago california wildfires Jun 10 High fire risk could trigger PG&E power shutoffs in parts of Bay Area

headlines.title: Why Olympic gold medalist Shawn Johnson is in love with gymnastics all over again
headlines.description: Shawn Johnson reached her Olympic dreams at 16. Now, almost two decades later, she’s sharing laughter-filled videos of her family on social media (peppered with occasional feats of gymnastics) and helping athletes with intellectual disabilities reach their own gymnastics dreams at the Special Olympics. We spoke with her as she announced the release of a documentary that follows three of those athletes on their journey to the Special Olympics USA Games in Minneapolis in late June. Here’s a transcript of our interview with her, edited for length and clarity. Q: What is it about gymnastics that you think is so special? Shawn Johnson East: It was a sport that made me feel invincible. It gave me a confidence that I could translate and take with me in any area of life, but being able to somewhat fly and flip on a four-inch beam makes you feel pretty cool. Q: You and your husband have a fantastic presence on Instagram. Shawn Johnson East: My husband and I really enjoy being creative and having fun and laughing and posting lighthearted content that maybe makes someone smile. Q: How will you guys be making Father’s Day awesome? Shawn Johnson East: We’ll probably just spend the day at home as a family and shower him with arts and crafts, which my kids love to do, and playing outside. Q: Will you be playing any Jenga? Because I did see that video (of you balancing on top of a Jenga tower on one high-heeled foot). Shawn Johnson East: I don’t know, maybe we’ll play Jenga! Q: I think this shows that you still have some superhuman powers, even if you’re not in the gym every day? Shawn Johnson East: Yeah, I have maybe some odd powers. I don’t know if it’s superhuman. Q: Something else I’ve seen on your Instagram is you’ve recently rescued a dog. Shawn Johnson East: His name’s Oakley, and he’s obsessed with our children. They are best friends. They sleep together. They spend every second together. And he’s the joy of our life. We love rescuing dogs because they need homes. And they’re such a great addition to our family. He fits perfectly in our family, and we love him. Q: So, last night, I watched this film called “Soaring.” How did you get involved with the Special Olympics (as a gymnastics coach) and with the film? Shawn Johnson East: After I retired from the Olympics, I have been an ambassador and supporter of many different games. The Invictus Games, Wounded Warriors, Paralympics, Special Olympics. I love these girls (the three Special Olympians featured in “Soaring”), and to hear their nerves and worries are exactly the same as mine were, being able to relate to them on such a competitive, specific gymnastics level, made us feel like friends and sisters. They love gymnastics so much, and they’re not swept up in the politics or the fame or the medals or pressures or money. For me, getting to such a high level, that got kind of messy and confusing, so I lost a little bit of that love, and I really think these girls gave that back to me. Q: Now, we have another Olympics coming up after (the Special Olympics USA Games in June) — the LA Olympics (in 2028). Shawn Johnson East: LA, just being able to host such a big Olympics on U.S. Soil, I think is going to be really special. It’s a chance for us to unify the world on such a big athletic stage. Q: Is Simone Biles gonna make a comeback? Do you have any thoughts on that at all? Shawn Johnson East: I don’t know. That is completely up to her. I think she’s an icon of our sport, and she has nothing left to prove. So if she wants to return, that’s for her enjoyment, and for us to cheer her on.
